_note: Sun Ce's deathbed farewell to 'his wife, Lady Qiao' (Da Qiao)
'her sister... her husband, Zhou Yu' - Xiao Qiao is Zhou Yu's wife
Da Qiao and Xiao Qiao are sisters ('your sister will come to see you presently')

summary: The Daoist saint whose passing empties Sun Ce's banquet tower of its guests. Imprisoned for 'deluding the people', he wins the rain-prayer wager - three spans of rain from a cloudless sky - and is beheaded anyway. His body vanishes in a storm, and his wraith haunts Sun Ce in mirror, monastery smoke, gate, and flame until the young conqueror's wounds burst and kill him.
_note: summary was missing for the chapter's central figure

summary: Sun Ce's wife (the prose calls her Lady Qiao), summoned to the deathbed: care for his mother, and pass through her sister the charge that Zhou Yu must uphold Sun Quan.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Quoted twice by Lady Wu on the reality of spiritual beings - her scripture for making Sun Ce pray at the Jade Pure Monastery after Yu Ji's death.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Duke Wen, one of the two Protectors of Reign Sun Quan names to Lu Su in the midnight talk as his models for leading the feudal lords.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Duke Huan, the other Protector of Reign in Sun Quan's midnight ambition - the hegemon model Lu Su gently talks him past, toward empire itself.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Receives Sun Ce's victory memorial - and the petition Cao Cao later sends conferring on Sun Quan the title of General and Governor of Kuaiji.
_note: summary was missing

summary: The Latter Han founder in Ma Yuan's maxim, quoted by Zhou Yu to Lu Su: ministers must choose their princes as princes choose their ministers.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Governor of Lujiang (the prose spells him Liu Xu), defeated by Sun Ce in AD 199 - the conquest that opens the chapter's account of southern supremacy.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Lu Su's friend who had engaged him to join Zheng Bao at Chaohu - the rival offer Zhou Yu talks him out of with Ma Yuan's maxim.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Proposes the rain-prayer wager as an amercement for the condemned Yu Ji - 'it is very dry just now'.
_note: summary was missing; NOTE this row appears to duplicate Lü Fan [1554] (Sun Ce's adviser since ch15) - flagged in audit_notes.md

summary: The Han general whose maxim - 'ministers must also choose their princes' - Zhou Yu quotes to pull Lu Su away from Chaohu.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Invoked twice: Xu Gong's secret letter brands Sun Ce 'a turbulent fellow of the Xiang Yu type', and Lu Su casts Cao Cao as the Xiang Yu blocking any true service to the Emperor.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Emperor Yi, whom the Han founder wished to serve but could not for Xiang Yu's evildoing - Lu Su's parallel for the Emperor caged by Cao Cao.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Invoked in the verse on Xu Gong's three retainers: loyal avengers of a murdered lord, self-immolated like Yu Rang of old.
_note: summary was missing

summary: The Imperial Protector of Jiaozhou cited in Sun Ce's tirade against superstition: deluded by red-turbaned doctrine into drum-beating faith, and slain by the enemy anyway.
_note: summary was missing

summary: The minister whose physiognomy reading haunts the succession: of all the Sun brothers, only Sun Quan - remarkable face, unusual build - has the look of great honor and long life.
_note: old summary: 'minor figure named in passing'

summary: The Chaohu leader Lu Su had half-agreed to join before Zhou Yu redirected him to Sun Quan.
_note: old summary: 'minor figure named in passing'

summary: Governor of Yuzhang who surrenders to Sun Ce at the mere arrival of Yu Fan's dispatch - the bloodless conquest that rounds out the southern domain.
_note: old summary made him Cao Cao's envoy; in ch29 he is the surrendering governor

summary: His verdict on Sun Ce - frivolous, unready, 'a stupid swaggerer who will die at the hands of some mean person' - is repeated to the wounded conqueror's face by Zhang Hong, and comes true within the chapter.
_note: old summary gave him the recognition advice; his role is the fulfilled prophecy

summary: Returns from the capital with Guo Jia's insult ringing; after Sun Ce's death he talks Cao Cao out of attacking mourners - 'treat him generously' - and comes home bearing Sun Quan's title of General and Governor of Kuaiji, joining Zhang Zhao in the administration and recruiting Gu Yong.
_note: old summary was a bare 'two Zhangs' mention

summary: Named as the Historian whose disciple Gu Yong was - the pedigree behind Sun Quan's correct, abstemious new Deputy Governor.
_note: old summary was vague literary-aside filler

summary: Arrives with Yuan Shao's alliance offer in time to watch his host arrest a saint mid-banquet; he is finally sent home with dispatches breaking off all negotiations - and his report sets Yuan Shao marching seven hundred thousand men toward Xuchang.
_note: old summary missed that his embassy fails

summary: Zhuge Liang's elder brother, recommended by Lu Su and treated as a superior guest; his first counsel steers the new regime - refuse Yuan Shao, lean toward Cao Cao until occasion serves.
_note: old summary omitted the advice that decides the chapter's diplomacy

summary: Pleads for the imprisoned saint her son mocks, quotes Confucius to force the monastery pilgrimage, and presides over the deathbed - confirming to Sun Quan that his gifts surpass his brother's, and pointing him to Zhou Yu and Zhang Zhao.
_note: old summary had only the succession scene

summary: His dispatch alone brings the Governor of Yuzhang, Hua Xin, over without a fight.
_note: old summary was a bare court mention

summary: His envoy Chen Zhen returns with the alliance dead and Cao Cao's titles draped over the new southern lord - and Yuan Shao, enraged, begins mustering seven hundred thousand troops against Xuchang: the road to Guandu.
_note: old summary was a bare off-stage mention
